segments.s516-g25: This clause states a comparison with an implied copula (هستی) omitted for poetic concision: 'تو از من ... برتری' = 'you are superior to me'. The preposition از introduces the standard of comparison ('than/me'), and به هر باره‌ای ('in every respect/occasion') qualifies the scope of that superiority.
segments.s516-g26: روان را is the definite object (را marks the direct object) and refers to 'the soul/spirit.' به + دانش indicates the instrument or means ('by/with knowledge'). همی‌پروری is a continuous/present-form verb (poetic formation with همی-) meaning 'you keep nurturing / you nourish continually.' The whole clause = 'you continually nourish the soul with knowledge.'
segments.s516-g27: بدین هرچ گفتی functions as an ablative/adverbial phrase ('by/with whatever you have said'). The main predication is مرا راه نیست: here a verbless existential construction uses نیست to state lack of a way for me — 'I have no path/way.' Note the poetic compression: phrases that would be full clauses in colloquial Persian are condensed.
segments.s516-g28: خور و ماه is a coordinate subject 'the sun and the moon.' زین (archaic/poetic contraction equivalent to 'از این') links to دانش ('of/pertaining to this knowledge'). آگاه نیست is a negated adjectival predicate meaning 'are not aware/know nothing of it.'
segments.s516-g29: This is a noun-list with a 2nd-person possessor: خور و خواب و رای و نشست ترا = 'your eating, your sleeping, your counsel, and your gatherings.' The enclitic ت (here as part of ترا) marks second-person possession and را is the definite/object marker attached for emphasis; the list is the topic of the following predication.
segments.s516-g30: به نیک و به بد are prepositional phrases meaning 'toward good and toward bad.' راه و دست ترا — again possessed by the 2nd-person suffix (ت) and emphasized with را — names the instruments/effects ('your way and your power'); together the phrase says these habits and counsels form the means by which you go to good or evil.
segments.s516-g31: ازان خواه راهت is an imperative/request construction: 'choose/seek your way from that (source).' خواه is a desiderative/imperative verb here ('seek/want'). راهت ('your way') is the possessed noun. The relative clause introduced by که ('who/that') attaches to راه and gives the agent: 'the One who created the Way.'
segments.s516-g32: This final clause lists cosmic creations as evidence of that Creator's power: شب و روز و خورشید و ماه آفرید = 'He created night and day and the sun and the moon.' The verb آفرید (past) names the creative action that the previous relative clause ascribed to the Maker.
segments.s516-g17: هرانگه که introduces a temporal subordinate clause (‘whenever/when’) placed before the main clause. زین is an archaic/poetic contraction equivalent to از این (“from/through this”), and تیرگی is the noun ‘darkness/obscurity.’ بگذرم is a first-person singular present-subjunctive/optative form used here with a future sense ‘I pass / I get through.’ The whole segment sets the time for the following statement.
segments.s516-g18: بگویم is a first-person form meaning ‘I will say / I will tell’ (classical Persian uses the present-subjunctive form with future/intentional sense). جفای تو is the direct object ‘your injustice/cruelty.’ با introduces the indirect object or beneficiary/instrument, and داورم (داور + enclitic -م) means ‘my judge/arbiter’ (here the addressee to whom the speaker will complain). The clause is the main clause following the temporal clause.
segments.s516-g19: بنالم is first-person singular present-subjunctive/optative with an intentive meaning ‘I will lament / I will bewail.’ ز تو means ‘because of you / about you.’ پیش یزدان پاک places the action before the pure God (پیش = ‘before, in the presence of’). The segment states the action and its audience.
segments.s516-g20: This is a compact, descriptive follow-up: خروشان is an adverbial/participial adjective ‘roaring’ or ‘with a tumultuous voice.’ به‌سربر is a poetic compound meaning roughly ‘on the head / over the head’ (here indicating the posture or location of what follows). پراگنده modifies خاک (‘dust’) as an adjective ‘scattered, strewn.’ Together the phrase vividly describes the speaker’s lamenting posture — head scattered upon the dust, uttering a roar.
segments.s516-g21: چنین داد پاسخش is an inverted phrase where چنین (‘thus/so’) modifies داد (‘gave’) and پاسخ is the direct object: ‘thus gave answer.’ سپهر بلند (‘the high/lofty heaven’) is the agent (subject) placed after the verb in typical poetic word order, so the clause means ‘thus the lofty heaven answered.’
segments.s516-g22: که‌ای here is a vocative/exclamatory address (classical poetic form equivalent to ‘که ای’ = ‘O’ + pronoun), introducing direct speech. مرد گوینده is the addressee ‘man, the speaker’ and بی‌گزند is an adjective ‘unharmed / without harm.’ The phrase is the content of the celestial reply, addressing the human interlocutor.
segments.s516-g23: چرا is the interrogative ‘why.’ بینی is the second-person singular present ‘you see / you perceive.’ از من here means ‘about me / in me.’ همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ‘still / always.’ نیک و بد is a coordination ‘good and bad.’ The clause asks why the addressee continually judges the speaker’s good and bad qualities.
segments.s516-g24: چنین ناله is ‘such a lament/complaint.’ از دانشی literally ‘from/of knowledge’ links the lament to the faculty or claim of knowledge. کی سزد is an impersonal interrogative ‘when / at what time is it fitting / appropriate?’ (سزد is the impersonal present meaning ‘it befits / is appropriate’). The whole asks rhetorically when such a lament is appropriate for one who has knowledge.
segments.s516-g9: پر از برف شد کوهسار سیاه is a typical Persian nominal phrase with the verb 'شد' (became) at the end: 'کوهسار سیاه' (the dark/high mountain) is the subject, and 'پر از برف شد' is a predicate meaning 'became full of snow'. Here پر is used adjectivally as 'full (of...)' followed by از + noun to indicate what it is full of. The verb شد is the past/effective 'became'.
segments.s516-g10: همی لشکر از شاه بیند گناه: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle here (adds the sense 'continually' or 'kept'); لشکر (the army/host) is subject, بیند is the present/past-visible form of 'to see' (here 'saw/perceived'); از شاه marks the source/target of the sight ('of the king' / 'in the king'), and گناه is the object ('guilt/fault'). Word order is S–(adverb)–(PP)–V–O, typical of poetic Persian where particles like همی precede the verb.
segments.s516-g11: به کردار مادر بدی تاکنون: به here introduces the manner ('in the manner of / by'), so 'به کردار' = 'in the conduct/behavior'. مادر is the possessor/agent phrase modifying کردار ('the mother's conduct'), بدی is a noun meaning 'evil/wickedness', and تاکنون 'until now/up to now' sets the temporal frame. Word order places the manner phrase before the time adverb for emphasis.
segments.s516-g12: همی ریخت باید ز رنج تو خون: همی again gives an ongoing/continuous nuance ('constantly/kept on'); ریخت here is the noun/verb sense 'pouring/flow' (i.e. blood pouring/flowing); باید is the modal 'must/ought' (necessity), so the clause says blood 'must have been pouring'. ز (poetic form of از) introduces the source/reason 'from your suffering' (رنج تو) and خون is the thing that poured. The sentence uses an older Poetic order with the modal before the source phrase.
segments.s516-g13: وفا و خرد نیست نزدیک تو: a simple negative copular clause. 'وفا و خرد' (fidelity and wisdom) is the compound subject; نیست is the negative copula 'is not / there is not'; نزدیک تو means 'near you' ('نزدیک' as a predicate adjective with the possessor 'تو'). The order Subject–NegCopula–Predicate is common in poetry for emphasis.
segments.s516-g14: پر از رنجم از رای تاریک تو: پر از + noun = 'full of ...'; here 'پر از رنجم' = 'full of my suffering' (رنجم = رنج + -م 'my'). The following از رای تاریک تو gives the reason/source: 'because of / from your dark counsel' (از = 'because of / from' in the second occurrence). رای here means 'plan/counsel/decision' and تاریک qualifies it. The clause thus links the speaker's being full of suffering to the addressee's dark counsel.
segments.s516-g15: مرا کاچ هرگز نپروردیی: مرا (object 'me') appears as م + را in the text's segmentation (را is the definite object marker placed after the clitic). کاچ is the vocative/name addressed. هرگز 'never' negates the verb. نپروردیی is the negative past second-person singular of پروردن ('you did not rear/raise'); the doubled یی ending is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person marker. The order (object + vocative + adverb + verb) is poetic but the grammatical roles are standard: Kaچ (subject/vocative) addressed, مرا = object, نپروردیی = verb 'you did not raise'.
segments.s516-g16: چو پرورده بودی نیازردیی: چو here = 'if/when' giving a conditional/hypothetical ('had you reared...'). پرورده is the past participle 'reared/nurtured' and بودی is the past auxiliary 'you had/you were' (forming pluperfect/conditional). نیازردیی is the negative past/conditional form 'you would not have hurt/grieved' (نی- negation + آزار/زرد root form in archaic conjugation). Together the clause reads, 'If you had reared (me), you would not have harmed me.'
segments.s516-g41: به is the preposition introducing the goal/direction ('toward/to'). یزدان is the divine addressee ('God'). گرای is an imperative/hortative form meaning 'turn/lean (toward)' in this poem (an older/poetic form of 'گرائ'/'گرا'); the verb is short and imperative-like. The second clause repeats the preposition + divine noun and uses the noun پناه ('refuge') elliptically (the verb 'take/seek' is omitted but understood): 'turn to God and (seek) refuge in God.'
segments.s516-g42: براندازه is an adverbial phrase 'to the extent / in due measure'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and links to the pronoun that follows (poetically written together as زو). هرچ ('whatever') is the indefinite object placed before the verb, and باید is a modal ('it is right / one must'). بخواه is the jussive/imperative/subjunctive verb 'ask/seek' (2nd person imperative or optative), and the verb-final position is normal Persian word order (object(s) before verb).
segments.s516-g43: جز او را is a fronted exception phrase 'except him' or 'none but him', with را marking او as the definite object/topic. مخوان is a negative imperative ('do not call/cry to' — classical/poetic negative form with the negative prefix m-). کردگار سپهر is an appositive epithet naming that 'him' — 'the Creator (کردگار) of the sky/heavens (سپهر)'. The verb-negative governs the prohibition; the epithets identify the permitted addressee.
segments.s516-g44: فروزنده is a participle/adjective 'illuminating' or 'one who makes bright' that modifies the following celestial names. ماه, ناهید, مهر are coordinated nouns ('the moon, Nahīd/Venus, and Mehr/Mithra'); the participle governs all three in a series. The conjunctions و simply link the items; note مهر here functions as the deity-name (Mithra) rather than the abstract 'love'.
segments.s516-g45: وزو here is a compact/poetic cluster (و + زو / و + زِ او) used to link clauses and indicate 'and upon him/for him' — this is an archaic/poetic contraction rather than the literal noun 'ablution'. بر is the preposition 'upon', روان is 'soul/spirit', محمد is the proper name (the Prophet), and درود is the noun 'blessing(s)/greeting'. Grammatically درود is placed before the verb that follows in the next segment; the verb that takes درود as object is deferred to the next clause.
segments.s516-g46: به یارانش is an indirect-object phrase 'to/for his companions' with the possessive suffix -ش ('his'). بر هر یکی literally 'upon each one' = 'for each of them'. برفزود is a past-tense verb 'he increased / he bestowed additionally' (بر- prefix + فزود), and it applies to the object درود from the previous segment: 'he increased the blessings for each of his companions.' Word order follows classical Persian: indirect object and adverbial before the verb-final form.
segments.s516-g1: الا‌ای is a poetic vocative ('O', 'lo!') addressing someone or something. برآورده is a participial adjective 'granting/fulfilling' modifying چرخ; چرخ here is the cosmic 'wheel' = Heaven/Fate, and بلند is an attributive adjective 'high/lofty' describing that sky/fate. Word order is emphatic and poetic: vocative + modifiers + head noun.
segments.s516-g2: چه داری is an exclamatory question: چه (here 'what'/'why' in an exclamative sense) + داری (2nd person singular present 'you have'/'what power/right do you have'). به پیری sets the temporal frame 'in/at old age'. مرا is the object pronoun 'me' and را is the object/definiteness marker attached to the object phrase; مستمند is a predicate/adjective 'needy', and the clause is elliptic/poetic (there is no explicit verb meaning 'to make', so داری functions to express 'what right/power have you to (leave/make) me needy').
segments.s516-g3: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بودم is 1st-person past 'I was' and together with جوان ('young') yields 'when I was young'. در برم is a locative/possessive phrase: در 'in' + برم (بر + -م) indicating 'in my bosom/under my care' or 'in my keeping'. داشتی is 2nd-person past imperfect 'you used to have/kept' — here 'you kept (me) in your care' with the subject omitted (understood 'you').
segments.s516-g4: به پیری again marks time 'in/at old age'. چرا is the interrogative 'why'. خوار is an adjective 'lowly/despised/humiliated'. بگذاشتی is 2nd-person past ('you put/left/allowed') and here carries the meaning 'you left/you caused (me) to be humiliated', so the clause asks why the addressee abandoned the speaker to humiliation in old age.
segments.s516-g5: همی is an archaic/poetic adverb here meaning roughly 'even so' or marking a general/habitual result. زرد گردد is a verb phrase: زرد 'yellow/pale' + گردد (classical subjunctive/optative of گشت) 'becomes/turns'. گل کامگار is the subject phrase 'the flower of the contented/satisfied one' (کامگار = 'satisfied/successful'); word order places the verb phrase before the subject for poetic effect.
segments.s516-g6: همی again marks the continuing or inevitable result. پرنیان is a noun 'silken robe' (literal fine garment). گردد 'becomes/is made' (same verbal root as above). از introduces the cause 'from/by'; رنج 'suffering/hardship' and خار 'thorn' together mean 'the suffering caused by thorns' — so the clause says even silk is spoiled by thorn-caused suffering. The verb is impersonal/neutral in form but acts on the subject 'پرنیان'.
segments.s516-g7: دو تا گشت is a compact numeric/verb phrase: دو تا 'two (units/pieces)' + گشت 'became/turned', so 'became two' or 'there were two'. آن سرو نازان is a demonstrative + noun phrase 'that coquettish cypress' (سرو = cypress, نازان = coquettish/dainty). به باغ is a locative 'in the garden'. The clause is terse and poetic; the verb 'گشت' applies to the preceding numeral phrase.
segments.s516-g8: همان 'the same' or 'likewise' connects to the previous clause. تیره گشت is 'turned dark/bleak' (تیره = dark/ominous, گشت = became). آن گرامی چراغ is a demonstrative + epithet + noun 'that honored/noble lamp' — چراغ is a poetic metaphor for a beloved or luminous person. The clause states that the lamp, too, became dark, in parallel with the cypress.
segments.s516-g33: یکی آنک introduces an existential/indefinite subject: 'there is one who...'. آنک is a classical relative 'that/which/who' attaching to یکی. هستیش is هست + ـیش, a nominalized/possessive form meaning 'his/its being' or 'existence'. را marks راز as the definite object of the clause, and نیست negates the whole object phrase: 'of his being there is no secret' => 'his existence has no secret.' Note the classical word order: subject + (possessive phrase + object) + predicate.
segments.s516-g34: به + کاریش sets up a relation 'with/regarding his work' (کاریش = کار + ـش 'his work/deed'). فرجام و آغاز is a conjoined noun phrase 'end and beginning' and نیست negates both members: 'there is no end and no beginning' for his work. Classical Persian often places the negator after the nouns it negates.
segments.s516-g35: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. گوید is third-person present of گفتن 'he says' (note: the provided senses list did not include 'say'). بباش is an imperative/exclamatory form 'Be!' (poetic/archaic). آنچ is a relative pronoun 'that which/what'. خواهد is third-person present 'wills/desires'. بدست is an adverbial phrase 'into the hand / comes to pass / is obtained' (literally 'in the hand'), completing the clause 'what he wills is obtained.'
segments.s516-g36: کسی is an indefinite 'anyone'. کو here is an archaic/poetic relative or interrogative particle ('who/that') rather than 'where' (the provided senses did not match). جزین = 'except this' (limiting phrase). داند is present 'knows' (he/she/one knows). آن is the demonstrative 'that'. بیهده‌ست is predicate 'is pointless / is futile' (بـِ + هست). The whole clause says: 'Anyone who (apart from this) thinks he knows, that is pointless.'
segments.s516-g37: من is the subject 'I'. از داد is a prepositional phrase 'from/by justice' meaning 'by right' (از + داد). چون تو is a simile 'like you'. یکی بنده‌ام is the nominal predicate 'I am one/one single servant', with the copular ending -ام attached to بنده; word order is subject + qualifying prepositional phrase + simile + predicate.
segments.s516-g38: This line is elliptical/poetic: the ezāfe linking is omitted. Read as پرستندهٔ آفریننده‌ام — 'I am the worshipper/devotee of the Creator.' پرستنده is the noun 'worshipper/devotee' and آفریننده‌ام serves as the final predicate with the copular/possessive ending -ام, giving the meaning '(of) the Creator I am [a worshipper]'.
segments.s516-g39: نگردم is a negative first-person present/future form 'I do not/will not turn' (ن- + گردم). همی is a continuous/emphatic particle 'always/still' placed after the verb. جز introduces an exception 'except'. به فرمان اوی = 'by/according to his command' with به + noun and اوی as the archaic possessive 'his'. The clause = 'I will not turn except by His command.'
segments.s516-g40: نیارم here functions as a negative auxiliary meaning roughly 'I will not' (the exact lexical senses supplied did not match, so treated as idiomatic negative). It takes the infinitive گذشتن 'to pass/ to transgress' as its complement. ز is the poetic preposition 'from / beyond', and پیمان اوی = 'his covenant' (اوی = his). The phrase means 'I will not transgress / pass beyond his covenant.'

sentences.s516-s13: You are superior to me in every respect; you continually nurture the soul with knowledge.
sentences.s516-s14: By all that you said I have no way; the sun and the moon are not aware of this knowledge.
sentences.s516-s15: Your eating and sleeping and counsel and gatherings — your way and your power lead to good and to bad.
sentences.s516-s16: Choose your way from that Source: the One who made the Way — He created night and day and the sun and the moon.
sentences.s516-s9: When I pass through this darkness, I will tell my judge of your injustice.
sentences.s516-s10: I will lament about you before the pure God, roaring, my head strewn upon the dust.
sentences.s516-s11: Thus the high heaven gave answer: 'O man who speaks, unharmed!'
sentences.s516-s12: Why do you always perceive both good and bad in me; when is such a lamented complaint fitting for knowledge?
sentences.s516-s5: The dark mountain became filled with snow; the army continually saw the king's fault.
sentences.s516-s6: Because of the mother's evil conduct up to now, blood must continually have been pouring from your suffering.
sentences.s516-s7: Fidelity and wisdom are not near you; I am full of suffering because of your dark counsel.
sentences.s516-s8: You never reared me, Kač; if you had reared me, you would not have harmed me.
sentences.s516-s21: Turn to God and take refuge in God; ask of him whatever you need in proper measure.
sentences.s516-s22: Call upon none but Him, the Creator of the heavens, the illuminator of the moon, Anahīd, and Mehr.
sentences.s516-s23: And he sent blessings upon Muhammad's soul, and he increased them for each of his companions.
sentences.s516-s1: O lofty, grant-giving Heaven — what right have you to make me needy in my old age?
sentences.s516-s2: When I was young you kept me in your care; why then did you leave me humiliated in my old age?
sentences.s516-s3: The satisfied flower turns pale; the silken robe too is ruined by the pain of thorns.
sentences.s516-s4: That coquettish cypress in the garden became two; that noble lamp likewise turned dark.
sentences.s516-s17: There is One whose existence has no secret; his work has neither beginning nor end.
sentences.s516-s18: When He says “Be,” whatever He wills is brought to hand; anyone who thinks he knows anything beyond this is in error and that is pointless.
sentences.s516-s19: I am, by right (by justice), like you a single servant; (I am) a worshipper of the Creator.
sentences.s516-s20: I will not turn aside except by His command; I will not pass beyond His covenant.
